text:
Greetings!

Name is Aaron. I'm new to the community, introduced by a good friend. I'm the technical director at Singularity 6, a newish game studio. Prior to that I was at Riot Games for a long time and prior to that a number of non-game tech startups in the US and in China/Hong Kong. I got my start in open source contributions in the Java projects over at Apache.

When it comes to the future of programming, I'm particularly interested in (1) practical applications in game development, such as Unreal's blueprints and (2) future productive VR/AR desktop environments. To this day, I'm still most at home in Emacs.

text:
<https://kaleguy.github.io/leovue>
> *Leo* is a uniquely powerful and versatile outlining editor. It is open source and runs on Windows/Mac/Linux. <http://www.leoeditor.com/|More about Leo.>
> Leo allows you to create content items and display them in multiple hierarchies.
> *LeoVue* is a web app created with the <https://vuejs.org/|Vue.js> framework. Use LeoVue to create complex sites from Leo outlines. Add Vue components to content items. Assemble content items into multiple views and pages. Load sites and data into nodes.

text: This reminded me of a talk by Alan Kay where he takes one 'app' - an animation - and connects it to another app - a picture editor. By exposing the methods of the animation and live binding it to the editor, he is able to construct an ensemble where one frame of the animation can be live edited in the picture editor, while the animation is playing. The main point was that these 'apps' were not designed with each other in mind, yet were able to work together. These were not apps, really - they were just objects with views. For them to work together, they were both introspectable (could see their methods), built from the same substrate (same smalltalk system) and shared the notion of some types (e.g. a picture itself).

elements.text: This reminded me of a talk by Alan Kay where he takes one 'app' - an animation - and connects it to another app - a picture editor. By exposing the methods of the animation and live binding it to the editor, he is able to construct an ensemble where one frame of the animation can be live edited in the picture editor, while the animation is playing. The main point was that these 'apps' were not designed with each other in mind, yet were able to work together. These were not apps, really - they were just objects with views. For them to work together, they were both introspectable (could see their methods), built from the same substrate (same smalltalk system) and shared the notion of some types (e.g. a picture itself).

text: Another angle to think about this, which I really like - is 'universal hypermedia'. Instead of thinking of apps, we think of a virtual shared world of hypermedia objects. Not only are objects link-lable, even slices or points within an object (e.g. a snippet in an essay) are link-able. A time point within a video becomes a point within that hypermedia object.

elements.text: Another angle to think about this, which I really like - is 'universal hypermedia'. Instead of thinking of apps, we think of a virtual shared world of hypermedia objects. Not only are objects link-lable, even slices or points within an object (e.g. a snippet in an essay) are link-able. A time point within a video becomes a point within that hypermedia object.

text: I've been thinking about the same but using another metaphor, fractal applications. Applications all the way down! what if every single part of an application was also a self contained application, whatever view of the application you are using could be just a part of other applications for other people, applications have no root or center. Instead of getting rid of applications we make them reusable.

text:
Have you read the python zen?

```>>> import this
The Zen of Python, by Tim Peters

Beautiful is better than ugly.
Explicit is better than implicit.
Simple is better than complex.
Complex is better than complicated.
Flat is better than nested.
Sparse is better than dense.
Readability counts.
Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules.
Although practicality beats purity.
Errors should never pass silently.
Unless explicitly silenced.
In the face of ambiguity, refuse the temptation to guess.
There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.
Although that way may not be obvious at first unless you're Dutch.
Now is better than never.
Although never is often better than *right* now.
If the implementation is hard to explain, it's a bad idea.
If the implementation is easy to explain, it may be a good idea.
Namespaces are one honking great idea -- let's do more of those!```

text:
Ruby quote time...

Ruby is designed to be human-oriented. It reduces the burden of programming. It tries to push jobs back to machines. You can accomplish more tasks with less work, in smaller yet readable code. (Matz)

Ruby is a very free-form community.  Matz is the most benevolent dictator I've had the pleasure to work with, and most of the community are true free-thinking artists.  It's like the hippie commune of the language world.  (Charles Nutter)

Ruby seems so self-explanatory to me. It makes it almost boring; you try to focus on Ruby and you wind up talking about some problem domain instead of the language.  (Steve Yegge)

text: I have to admit that in my Ruby days, this was the only language where it happened to me surprisingly often that I would just write a piece of code without looking at the documentation thinking “I have no idea but I feel like this is how that should work” — and it did.

text:
Hi all, Martin here :slightly_smiling_face:!
During college I competed in algorithms, did internships in Google & Palantir and did my Master's thesis (MCS) in the field of bioinformatics (on the algorithmic side). After that I did a startup and worked on different projects, latest worked as a lead engineer for London-based bioinformatics startup (developing a web app/platform).
I am excited about open source, computer science, functional programming, emacs, startups.
Right now I am working with my twin brother on a programming language of our own that is specialized for developing web apps (I will write more about the language in the <#CCL5VVBAN|feedback> channel).
I found out about this community through google search, and I am really excited that I will be able to learn from (and hopefully also help) so many experienced people working on similar problems!

text:
*Feedback request:* brother and I just finished first prototype of Wasp - higher-level programming language for building web apps - would love to get your feedback!
Webpage (with demo): <https://wasp-lang.dev>, Github repo: <https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp> .
We are still very early / experimental, and current demo is yet far away from our vision, but it should illustrate some basic concepts.
Our main challenge is designing the language so it captures the correct abstractions and is easy to use, while also making it flexible enough to be useful. Plan for now is to find inspiration in current popular frameworks and similar solutions + guide development by specific use cases (we started with Todo App).
Thanks!

text: Ruby is so delightfully unsound: perpetually punting on precision and completeness, favoring a sort of joy and ease.

elements.text: Ruby is so delightfully unsound: perpetually punting on precision and completeness, favoring a sort of joy and ease.

text:
For example, here's one that broke me — just now.  After writing it thousands of times, I cannot type this idiom anymore:
```hash_mapping_x_to_fx = Hash[xs.map{|x| [x, f(x)]] ```
From now on, I'm going with:
```hash_mapping_x_to_fx = xs.hap{|x| f(x)}```
Remember "Ruby was a Lisp originally, in theory."  So the definition, for those unfamiliar, can be simple as:
```module Enumerable
  def hap
    Hash[map{|x| [x, yield(x)]}]
  end
end```

text: Hello. I'm a full stack developer working mostly with JavaScript. Maybe thanks to unix, npm, and functional programming, I'm obsessed with pushing the bar toward simple, modular, expressive, reusable code, but not necessarily any good at it. My primary goal in programming right now is to write or settle on a JavaScript FRP library. To that end, I came across a Future of Coding blog post that talked about Reflex and Hareactive, and that lead me here.

To that end, I came across a Future of Coding blog post that talked about Reflex and Hareactive, and that lead me here.

text: Interesting, thanks for sharing! I used to use Leo ages ago, but gave up on it at some point because OrgMode in Emacs was more convenient (mainly because I needed Emacs anyway). I was surprised to see that Leo is still alive (it was and still is an essentially one-person project), and even more to see that it is developing a tool ecosystem around it!

text: Hi everyone! :slightly_smiling_face: I work on developer tooling in my day job and poke slowly at better ways to program in my vanishingly small spare time. I've been interested in FoC-adjacent topics for a long time and eventually found my way here by way of following <@UCUSW7WVD>’s Mu progress on Mastodon.
